what_manipulated: subjective socioeconomic status (felt social standing via ladder task)
experiment: Participants ranked themselves on a ladder comparing to richer/poorer peers or control, then made meal choices, rated implicit food preferences, consumed snacks ad libitum, or ate a meal to satiation. All compared low vs. high vs. neutral socioeconomic status manipulations.

what_manipulated: temporal comparison direction (past vs. future environmental framing)
what_measured: environmental attitudes, proenvironmental behaviors, monetary donations
experiment: Participants read or viewed environment-related messages or photos comparing present to past (or future), then rated environmental attitudes or allocated donations to charities. A meta-analysis synthesized studies 1-6 for effect sizes across conditions and participant ideologies (conservative vs. liberal).

what_manipulated: presence and saliency of thank-you gifts in fundraising solicitations
experiment: Field: donors randomly assigned to receive standard solicitation letter with/without colorful gift insert (swag or prosocial meal gift) or control; donation rate by mail recorded. Online: participants rated likelihood of watching science videos paired with low/high visual salience gift images; donation to Red Cross measured.

what_manipulated: facial appearance traits (competence, morality, sociability, attractiveness) of scientists
what_measured: interest in scientist's work, perception of research quality, selection of science news items
experiment: Studies 1-2: Participants rated scientist faces on traits and judged interest/quality. Studies 3-4: Participants chose real science news paired with low/high interest-rated faces. Studies 5-6: Participants read real science news paired with low/high quality-rated faces, rated research quality. Predictors standardized; mixed-effects models estimated trait effects.

what_manipulated: belief in free will (weakened by reading anti-free-will passage vs. control text)
what_measured: correspondence bias (internal vs. external causal attribution), prescribed punishment/reward for others' behavior
experiment: Studies 1-2: Participants read essays on controversial issues, rated author's position; measured free will belief. Studies 3a-3b: Participants assigned to anti-free-will or control text, then rated causal attributions for scenario behaviors. Study 4: Measured correspondence bias, locus of control, judgment certainty; tested mediation to prescribed punishment/reward. Correlations and experimental effects reported.

what_manipulated: learning algorithm vulnerability and cost of punishment in stealing/punishment games
what_measured: persistence in rigid punishment vs. flexible theft, evolved intrinsic biases for stealing/punishing
experiment: Evolutionary game-theory analysis and simulations: modeled thief and victim with flexible/rigid strategies; Q-learning agents played repeated steal/punish game; Moran process simulated evolution over populations. Behavioral experiment: humans randomly assigned to thief/victim role, played repeated game against fixed opponents; tracked theft and punishment rates by round.
